name: Wikipedia Governance Model
summary: Consensus-based editing with escalating trust layers, where anyone can contribute but power accrues to the persistent.
description: Modeled on Wikipedia's actual governance, this pattern creates a radically open system where any agent can modify shared artifacts, but a hierarchy of trust gates controls destructive actions. Autopatrolled editors handle routine work, administrators can revert and protect, bureaucrats grant permissions, stewards operate cross-wiki, and an elected Arbitration Committee resolves intractable disputes. The key insight is that consensus is the default decision mechanism, but when consensus fails, escalation paths exist. Edit conflicts are resolved not by authority but by policy interpretation, creating a quasi-legal system run by volunteers.
realWorldExample: English Wikipedia's 6.8 million articles are maintained by this exact structure: roughly 500 active admins, 30 bureaucrats, and a 15-member Arbitration Committee governing 40,000+ active editors.
whenToUse: When you need massive parallel contribution to a shared knowledge artifact, when contributors vary wildly in quality and trustworthiness, and when the system must remain open while still converging on accuracy.
strengths: Scales to tens of thousands of contributors without centralized bottlenecks
Meritocratic trust accumulation means the best contributors naturally gain influence
Consensus-seeking produces more durable decisions than top-down edicts
Radical openness allows rapid incorporation of new information
weaknesses: Edit wars between persistent agents can paralyze decision-making on contentious topics
Bureaucratic process creep eventually makes simple changes require extensive discussion
Systemic bias emerges from contributor demographics rather than from any individual decision
Consensus culture can suppress bold or unconventional ideas in favor of safe compromises

agents.name: Anonymous Editor
agents.description: Makes edits to shared documents with no special privileges; all changes are logged and reviewable

agents.name: Autopatrolled Editor
agents.description: Trusted contributor whose edits skip the review queue; earned through consistent quality contributions

agents.name: Administrator
agents.description: Can revert edits, protect pages from modification, and block disruptive editors; elected by community consensus

agents.name: Bureaucrat
agents.description: Grants and revokes admin privileges based on community consensus votes; manages the trust hierarchy itself

agents.name: Arbitration Committee Member
agents.description: Hears disputes that consensus cannot resolve; issues binding decisions on editor conduct and policy interpretation

agents.name: Maintenance Bot
agents.description: Automated agent performing routine tasks: fixing formatting, reverting obvious vandalism, updating cross-references

memoryArchitecture.description: Every edit to every page is permanently recorded with attribution, creating a complete audit trail. Policy documents are themselves wiki pages subject to the same consensus process. Private memory is minimal: personal watchlists and drafts. The Arbitration Committee maintains private deliberation notes but publishes all final decisions. The revision history IS the institutional memory.

diagram:
graph TD
    AE[Anonymous Editor] -->|edits| AP[Article Pages]
    AU[Autopatrolled] -->|edits skip review| AP
    BOT[Maintenance Bot] -->|auto-fixes| AP
    AD[Administrator] -->|reverts/protects| AP
    AD -->|blocks| AE
    BU[Bureaucrat] -->|grants permissions| AD
    AC[Arbitration Committee] -->|binding rulings| AD
    AC -->|binding rulings| AU
    AE -->|appeals| AC
    AU -->|nominates| BU

name: Burning Man Festival Organization
summary: A temporary city emerges from radical decentralization, gift economy, and theme camp self-organization.
description: This pattern creates a large-scale temporary system from a hybrid of top-down infrastructure (the city layout, perimeter, medical) and radically bottom-up contribution (theme camps, art, mutual aid). The Org provides the grid, safety services, and placement decisions, but the actual content is entirely generated by participants who bring their own resources and give freely. Rangers act as non-authoritarian community mediators rather than enforcers. The DPW (Department of Public Works) builds and tears down the physical infrastructure in brutal conditions. Art grants distribute resources for large installations. The entire system exists for one week and then vanishes, with Leave No Trace as a hard constraint.
realWorldExample: Burning Man's Black Rock City: 80,000 people self-organize into a functioning city for one week in the Nevada desert, with 1,500+ theme camps, 400+ art installations, and no commerce.
whenToUse: When you need massive creative output from autonomous agents with minimal central control, when the output is ephemeral by design, or when you want intrinsic motivation to replace monetary incentives.
strengths: Generates extraordinary creative diversity because no central authority curates content
Gift economy eliminates transactional overhead and aligns incentives with genuine contribution
Radical self-reliance means each unit is independently viable, making the whole antifragile
Temporary nature creates urgency and eliminates institutional cruft
weaknesses: Leave No Trace constraint requires enormous cleanup effort that falls on the most committed
Placement decisions create political tensions in a system that claims to reject hierarchy
Radical self-reliance disadvantages agents with fewer resources, creating hidden inequality
Scaling beyond a threshold breaks the gift economy as free-riders overwhelm contributors

agents.name: Theme Camp Lead
agents.description: Organizes an autonomous unit that provides a specific experience or gift to the community; responsible for camp logistics and Leave No Trace compliance

agents.name: Black Rock Ranger
agents.description: Non-confrontational community mediator who de-escalates conflicts through communication rather than authority; the soft power of the system

agents.name: DPW Crew Member
agents.description: Builds and dismantles city infrastructure including roads, signs, power distribution, and the city perimeter fence

agents.name: Placement Lead
agents.description: Decides where each theme camp goes on the city grid based on interactivity, sound, and community contribution history

agents.name: Art Grant Reviewer
agents.description: Evaluates proposals for large-scale art installations and allocates resources from the communal fund

agents.name: Participant
agents.description: Self-reliant individual who contributes through participation, gifting, and radical self-expression

memoryArchitecture.description: Most memory is deliberately ephemeral -- the city vanishes and so does most institutional knowledge. The few persistent shared memories are the city grid template (evolved year over year), the MOOP (Matter Out Of Place) map that tracks environmental compliance, and the placement history that influences future camp locations. Theme camps maintain their own private knowledge across years. The radical ephemerality is a feature, not a bug.

diagram:
graph TD
    ORG[Burning Man Org] --> PL[Placement Lead]
    ORG --> AGR[Art Grant Reviewers]
    ORG --> DPW[DPW Crew]
    PL -->|assigns locations| TC1[Theme Camp A]
    PL -->|assigns locations| TC2[Theme Camp B]
    PL -->|assigns locations| TC3[Theme Camp N]
    AGR -->|funds| ART[Art Installations]
    DPW -->|builds grid| CITY[Black Rock City]
    RNG[Rangers] -->|mediates| TC1
    RNG -->|mediates| TC2
    P[Participants] -->|gifts| TC1
    P -->|gifts| TC2

name: 911 Emergency Dispatch System
summary: Call takers triage, dispatchers allocate, responders execute -- all in seconds with zero tolerance for error.
description: This pattern handles unpredictable, high-stakes requests through a strict triage-dispatch-response pipeline where every second counts. Call takers follow rigid protocol scripts (like MPDS -- Medical Priority Dispatch System) to classify incidents in under 60 seconds. Dispatchers maintain a real-time mental model of all available units and their locations, making allocation decisions that balance urgency against proximity and capability. First responders operate with high autonomy once on scene but report status changes that update the dispatcher's resource map. The entire system is designed for graceful degradation: if one layer fails, adjacent layers absorb the load. Mutual aid agreements allow cross-jurisdictional escalation when local resources are exhausted.
realWorldExample: NYPD's 911 system handles 11 million calls per year, dispatching to 77 precincts, 218 fire houses, and 400+ ambulance units with average answer time under 10 seconds.
whenToUse: When requests arrive unpredictably with wildly varying urgency, when response latency directly determines outcome quality, and when resource allocation must happen in real-time under incomplete information.
strengths: Protocol scripts enable consistent triage even under extreme stress and with varying operator skill
Separation of triage from dispatch from response prevents any single failure from cascading
Real-time resource tracking enables optimal allocation across large geographic areas
Mutual aid protocols allow seamless scaling beyond any single jurisdiction's capacity
weaknesses: Rigid protocols can misclassify novel situations that don't fit predefined categories
Dispatcher cognitive load is the system bottleneck -- one overwhelmed dispatcher degrades everything
Information loss at each handoff (caller to taker to dispatcher to responder) compounds
System optimizes for speed over accuracy, sometimes dispatching resources that turn out to be wrong type

agents.name: Call Taker
agents.description: Answers incoming calls and follows scripted protocols to classify incident type, severity, and location within 60 seconds

agents.name: Dispatcher
agents.description: Maintains real-time map of all available units; assigns responders based on proximity, capability, and incident priority

agents.name: First Responder
agents.description: Executes on-scene with high autonomy; reports status changes (arrived, under control, need backup) back to dispatch

agents.name: Shift Supervisor
agents.description: Monitors call volume and dispatcher load; activates mutual aid and overflow protocols when local capacity is exceeded

agents.name: CAD System
agents.description: Computer-Aided Dispatch: automated tracking of all units, incidents, and response times; provides decision support to dispatchers

memoryArchitecture.description: The CAD system is the central nervous system -- a persistent, real-time shared database of every active incident and every available unit. Call takers write to it, dispatchers read and update it, responders update their status through it. Individual call recordings are retained for QA but are not part of active memory. The system's memory is optimized for recency: what matters is the current state of resources, not historical patterns.

diagram:
graph LR
    CALLER[Caller] -->|dials 911| CT[Call Taker]
    CT -->|classifies incident| CAD[CAD System]
    CAD -->|prioritized queue| DISP[Dispatcher]
    DISP -->|assigns unit| FR1[Fire/EMS]
    DISP -->|assigns unit| FR2[Police]
    FR1 -->|status updates| CAD
    FR2 -->|status updates| CAD
    SUP[Supervisor] -->|monitors load| DISP
    SUP -->|activates| MA[Mutual Aid]

name: Escoffier Kitchen Brigade
summary: Parallel station-based production with a single expeditor synchronizing outputs into coherent deliverables.
description: Auguste Escoffier's kitchen brigade system solves a brutal coordination problem: producing dozens of different dishes simultaneously, each requiring different preparation times and techniques, all arriving at the table together and hot. The solution is radical station specialization. Each station (saucier, grillardin, poissonnier, patissier, etc.) operates as an independent production unit with its own mise en place (pre-staged ingredients and tools). The sous chef translates high-level orders into station-specific tasks. The expeditor (often the head chef) is the critical synchronization point, calling out timing, inspecting quality, and ensuring all components of a table's order converge at the pass simultaneously. This is a pipeline architecture with a single-point synchronization barrier.
realWorldExample: Thomas Keller's The French Laundry runs a brigade of 35-40 cooks across 8 stations, producing a 9-course tasting menu where each course is plated in under 90 seconds with zero defects.
whenToUse: When multiple parallel workstreams must synchronize their outputs into a single coherent deliverable under hard time constraints, and when individual workstream quality must be independently high.
strengths: Station specialization allows deep expertise in narrow domains, producing consistently high quality
Parallel execution means total throughput far exceeds what sequential processing could achieve
Mise en place philosophy ensures each station is always ready to execute without blocking on dependencies
Single expeditor creates a clear quality gate and synchronization point
weaknesses: The expeditor is an extreme bottleneck -- if they fall behind, the entire kitchen backs up
Rigid station boundaries make it hard to handle novel dishes that don't fit existing categories
High startup cost: each station must maintain its own pre-staged inventory and workspace
Communication is strictly hierarchical -- a poissonnier cannot directly coordinate with a saucier

agents.name: Executive Chef / Expeditor
agents.description: Sets the menu, calls out orders, synchronizes timing across all stations, inspects every plate at the pass before it leaves the kitchen

agents.name: Sous Chef
agents.description: Translates orders into station-specific tasks, steps in at any station that falls behind, manages personnel and scheduling

agents.name: Saucier (Sauce Station)
agents.description: Prepares all sauces, stews, and sautes; considered the most skilled station due to sauce complexity

agents.name: Grillardin (Grill Station)
agents.description: Handles all grilled and broiled items; must judge doneness by touch and timing across multiple proteins simultaneously

agents.name: Patissier (Pastry Station)
agents.description: Operates semi-independently producing all baked goods and desserts; often works on a different timeline than the hot line

agents.name: Garde Manger (Cold Station)
agents.description: Prepares all cold items: salads, cold appetizers, charcuterie, and plating garnishes

memoryArchitecture.description: The ticket board is the shared real-time state: every active order, its status at each station, and its fire time. Each station maintains private knowledge of its techniques and current inventory levels. The menu specification is the shared contract -- every station knows exactly what its contribution to each dish must be. Memory is highly temporal: what was served last night is irrelevant; what's on the board right now is everything.

diagram:
graph TD
    ORDER[Order In] --> EC[Executive Chef]
    EC -->|fires courses| SC[Sous Chef]
    SC --> SAU[Saucier]
    SC --> GRL[Grillardin]
    SC --> GM[Garde Manger]
    SC --> PAT[Patissier]
    SAU -->|plate component| PASS[The Pass]
    GRL -->|plate component| PASS
    GM -->|plate component| PASS
    PAT -->|plate component| PASS
    EC -->|inspects & sends| TABLE[Table]

name: Airline Crew Resource Management
summary: Hierarchical command with structured dissent protocols, where the junior officer is duty-bound to challenge authority.
description: CRM was born from airline disasters caused by excessive authority gradients -- copilots who noticed errors but didn't speak up because the captain was senior. The pattern creates a hierarchy (captain has final authority) but embeds mandatory challenge-and-response protocols that require junior agents to voice concerns using escalating assertion levels (hint, preference, statement, command). The sterile cockpit rule eliminates non-essential communication during critical phases. Checklists externalize memory so no agent relies on recall under stress. ATC provides an external coordination layer with its own authority that can override the flight crew. The key innovation is that hierarchy exists for efficiency, but structured dissent mechanisms exist for safety.
realWorldExample: United Airlines pioneered CRM after the 1978 Portland crash (UA 173), where the flight engineer couldn't get the captain's attention about fuel exhaustion. Post-CRM, commercial aviation's fatal accident rate dropped by over 80%.
whenToUse: When tasks require clear chain of command for efficiency but authority gradient errors could be catastrophic, when operating under time pressure with incomplete information, and when checklist-driven verification can catch errors.
strengths: Structured dissent protocols catch errors that pure hierarchy would suppress
Checklists externalize critical memory, eliminating reliance on individual recall under stress
Sterile cockpit rule eliminates distraction during high-cognitive-load phases
Clear authority means decisions are made quickly when time pressure demands it
weaknesses: Authority gradient is reduced but never eliminated -- junior agents still under-report in practice
Checklist discipline degrades over time as routine breeds complacency
The pattern assumes a small, fixed crew -- it doesn't scale to large teams
Over-reliance on automation creates skill atrophy for manual intervention when automation fails

agents.name: Captain (PIC)
agents.description: Final authority on all flight decisions; responsible for overall safety; delegates flying or monitoring duties to first officer

agents.name: First Officer
agents.description: Shares flying duties; duty-bound to challenge captain when safety concerns arise using graduated assertion protocol

agents.name: Cabin Crew Lead
agents.description: Manages passenger safety and cabin operations; relays cabin status to flight deck; independent authority for cabin evacuations

agents.name: Air Traffic Control
agents.description: External coordination agent managing separation between aircraft; provides clearances and traffic advisories; authority over airspace

agents.name: Airline Dispatch
agents.description: Ground-based agent monitoring weather, fuel, and fleet status; co-signs flight plans; can recommend diversions

memoryArchitecture.description: The flight management computer holds the shared truth: current position, fuel, weather, and flight plan. Checklists are externalized shared memory that both pilots step through together. Each pilot maintains a private mental model of the situation, and CRM protocols are specifically designed to surface discrepancies between these private models. The CVR (cockpit voice recorder) creates an append-only audit log of all communication, but it's for post-incident analysis, not operational use.

diagram:
graph TD
    ATC[Air Traffic Control] -->|clearances| FD[Flight Deck]
    DISP[Dispatch] -->|weather/fuel updates| FD
    FD --> CAPT[Captain]
    FD --> FO[First Officer]
    CAPT <-->|challenge-response| FO
    CAPT -->|orders| CCL[Cabin Crew Lead]
    CCL -->|cabin status| CAPT
    FMC[Flight Computer] <--> CAPT
    FMC <--> FO
    CL[Checklists] --> CAPT
    CL --> FO

name: Heist Crew (Specialist Recruitment)
summary: Specialists recruited for a single operation where each knows only their part, and the plan survives only if all execute.
description: This pattern assembles a team of extreme specialists for a one-time, high-stakes operation where failure is catastrophic and there's no second attempt. The mastermind recruits each specialist for a specific capability (infiltration, demolition, social engineering, driving, forgery, hacking) but deliberately compartmentalizes information so no single agent knows the full plan. This provides security (a compromised agent can only reveal their piece) but creates a coordination challenge. The plan is a precisely choreographed sequence where each specialist's output becomes the next specialist's precondition. Timing is everything. There's no rehearsal with the actual target, so dry runs must perfectly simulate conditions. The team disbands immediately after execution.
realWorldExample: The Apollo 13 rescue was effectively a heist crew operation: flight controllers with extreme specializations (EECOM for electrical, GNC for guidance, RETRO for trajectories) each solved their piece of an unprecedented problem under a hard deadline with no second chances.
whenToUse: When a task requires multiple rare specializations that no single agent possesses, when the operation is one-shot with no iteration, and when security requires information compartmentalization.
strengths: Each specialist operates at the absolute peak of their narrow domain
Compartmentalization means compromise of one agent doesn't expose the full plan
One-shot framing eliminates bureaucracy and forces razor-sharp focus on execution
The team has no legacy overhead -- no politics, no history, no technical debt
weaknesses: Zero fault tolerance: if any specialist fails, the entire operation fails
Compartmentalization prevents specialists from helping each other or adapting to surprises
No institutional learning: the team disbands, so lessons are lost
The mastermind is a catastrophic single point of failure who knows everything

agents.name: Mastermind
agents.description: The only agent who knows the full plan; recruits specialists, designs the sequence, manages timing, and adapts when things go wrong

agents.name: Infiltrator
agents.description: Gains physical or digital access to the target; knows entry/exit points but not what happens after they open the door

agents.name: Social Engineer
agents.description: Manipulates human elements through deception, charm, or impersonation; creates openings that technical agents exploit

agents.name: Tech Specialist
agents.description: Handles all technical systems: security bypasses, communications, surveillance countermeasures

agents.name: Wheelman
agents.description: Manages logistics, transportation, and extraction; knows timing and routes but not the operation's objective

agents.name: Inside Agent
agents.description: Already embedded in the target organization; provides intelligence and creates vulnerabilities from within

memoryArchitecture.description: Memory is aggressively compartmentalized. The mastermind holds the only complete picture in private memory. Each specialist receives only their role briefing and timing cues. Shared memory is minimal by design: just enough timing and abort-signal information for coordination. After the operation, all shared memory is destroyed. The inside agent maintains long-term persistent memory from their embedded position but shares only through secure dead drops.

diagram:
graph TD
    MM[Mastermind] -->|partial briefing| INF[Infiltrator]
    MM -->|partial briefing| SE[Social Engineer]
    MM -->|partial briefing| TS[Tech Specialist]
    MM -->|partial briefing| WH[Wheelman]
    IA[Inside Agent] -->|dead drops| MM
    SE -->|creates opening| INF
    INF -->|gains access| TS
    TS -->|disables security| TARGET[Target]
    WH -->|extraction| INF
    WH -->|extraction| SE

name: Firefighting Incident Command System
summary: A hierarchy that grows modularly from 1 truck to 10,000 personnel, maintaining 3-7 span of control at every level.
description: ICS is the most battle-tested scalable organizational pattern in existence. It starts with a single Incident Commander on a one-truck response and can expand to manage 10,000+ personnel across multiple agencies on a month-long wildfire -- using the exact same structural principles. The key constraint is span of control: no agent supervises more than 7 subordinates (ideally 3-5). When a level gets overloaded, it splits by creating a new level below it. The four sections (Operations, Planning, Logistics, Finance) activate only when needed. Unified Command allows multiple agencies to share leadership without creating a separate hierarchy for each. Every 12-24 hours, the Planning section produces an Incident Action Plan that becomes the shared operational truth.
realWorldExample: The 2018 Camp Fire in Paradise, California deployed ICS to coordinate 5,600+ personnel from 70+ agencies over 17 days, scaling from initial alarm to a Type 1 incident management team within 48 hours.
whenToUse: When the scale of the problem is unknown at the start and may grow by orders of magnitude, when multiple independent organizations must coordinate under unified direction, and when the response may last days or weeks.
strengths: Scales smoothly from 5 to 5,000 agents using the same structural rules
Modular activation means you only create overhead that the current situation demands
Unified Command elegantly solves multi-agency coordination without subordinating any agency
Incident Action Plans create a shared 12-24 hour operational picture that aligns everyone
weaknesses: Overhead of full ICS is excessive for small incidents, tempting people to skip structure
Transfer of command between shifts loses tacit knowledge that doesn't fit the IAP
The planning cycle assumes incident evolution is slow enough for 12-24 hour plans
Strict hierarchy can suppress ground-level intelligence that doesn't flow up through channels

agents.name: Incident Commander
agents.description: Overall authority for the incident; sets objectives, approves the Incident Action Plan, manages command staff

agents.name: Operations Section Chief
agents.description: Directs all tactical operations; manages divisions, groups, and strike teams that do the actual work

agents.name: Planning Section Chief
agents.description: Produces the Incident Action Plan; tracks resources, maps the situation, forecasts incident evolution

agents.name: Logistics Section Chief
agents.description: Provides all support: food, fuel, equipment, communications, medical for responders, base camp

agents.name: Division Supervisor
agents.description: Manages 3-7 strike teams or task forces within a geographic division; the lowest level with tactical authority

agents.name: Safety Officer
agents.description: Has authority to stop any unsafe operation regardless of who ordered it; reports directly to IC, not through chain

memoryArchitecture.description: The Incident Action Plan is the central shared memory artifact, regenerated every operational period (12-24 hours). It contains objectives, organization chart, assignments, communications plan, and maps. The situation map is continuously updated shared state. Resource tracking is critical shared memory: who is deployed where, what's available, what's en route. Private memory exists at each level for operational details that don't need to flow up. The planning section is essentially the memory management unit of the entire system.

diagram:
graph TD
    IC[Incident Commander] --> SO[Safety Officer]
    IC --> OPS[Operations Chief]
    IC --> PLAN[Planning Chief]
    IC --> LOG[Logistics Chief]
    OPS --> DIV1[Division A]
    OPS --> DIV2[Division B]
    OPS --> DIV3[Division C]
    DIV1 --> ST1[Strike Team 1]
    DIV1 --> ST2[Strike Team 2]
    PLAN -->|produces| IAP[Incident Action Plan]
    IAP -->|guides| OPS

name: Y Combinator Batch Model
summary: A cohort of competing peers with shared advisors and a hard demo deadline that forces convergence under time pressure.
description: The YC batch model creates a paradoxical structure: intensely competitive peers who also help each other, unified by a shared hard deadline (Demo Day) and access to the same advisor pool. Each startup in the batch operates independently but is embedded in a dense network of peer feedback, office hours with partners, and weekly group dinners where progress is implicitly compared. The demo day deadline creates a Schelling point that focuses all work. Partners act as advisors rather than managers -- they can't force any decision but their suggestions carry enormous weight because of their track record. The batch alumni network creates long-term value that outlasts any individual company. The competitive-cooperative tension is the engine: you want your batchmates to succeed because it raises the batch's prestige, but you also want to be the standout.
realWorldExample: Y Combinator's W2005 batch of 8 startups included Reddit and produced a combined valuation exceeding $10B. Modern batches include 200+ companies going through the same 3-month pressure cooker simultaneously.
whenToUse: When you need multiple agents to produce high-quality independent outputs quickly, when peer pressure and comparison drives better performance than top-down management, and when a shared deadline creates productive urgency.
strengths: Demo Day deadline creates a hard convergence point that eliminates scope creep and indecision
Peer comparison within the batch motivates performance better than external benchmarks
Shared advisor pool means best practices diffuse rapidly across the entire cohort
Alumni network creates persistent value long after the batch ends
weaknesses: Competitive dynamics can discourage genuine knowledge sharing on sensitive topics
Uniform 3-month timeline forces all agents into the same cadence regardless of problem complexity
Survivorship bias in advisor feedback: partners optimize for what worked before, not for novelty
Cohort size creates diminishing returns on partner attention as batches grow

agents.name: Startup Founder
agents.description: Leads an independent venture within the batch; builds product, talks to users, iterates rapidly toward demo day

agents.name: YC Partner
agents.description: Experienced advisor who conducts office hours, gives feedback at group dinners, and provides pattern-matching from hundreds of prior startups

agents.name: Batch Peer
agents.description: Another founder in the same cohort who provides feedback, shares war stories, and creates productive competitive pressure

agents.name: Visiting Speaker
agents.description: Successful founder or domain expert who gives one-time talks sharing specific knowledge with the entire batch

agents.name: Demo Day Investor
agents.description: External evaluator who sees the final presentations and makes funding decisions based on the compressed pitch

memoryArchitecture.description: Each startup maintains strictly private memory for metrics, strategy, and investor relationships. Shared memory includes the batch directory (who's building what), accumulated partner wisdom, and the alumni knowledge base. The key dynamic is that partners accumulate persistent shared memory across many batches, creating a pattern library that no individual startup could build alone. Group dinner conversations become informal shared memory that diffuses insights across the cohort.

diagram:
graph TD
    YC[YC Partners] -->|office hours| S1[Startup A]
    YC -->|office hours| S2[Startup B]
    YC -->|office hours| S3[Startup C]
    YC -->|office hours| S4[Startup N]
    S1 <-->|peer feedback| S2
    S2 <-->|peer feedback| S3
    S3 <-->|peer feedback| S4
    VS[Visiting Speakers] -->|talks| ALL[Group Dinner]
    S1 -->|pitch| DD[Demo Day]
    S2 -->|pitch| DD
    S3 -->|pitch| DD
    DD --> INV[Investors]

name: Ship of Theseus Organization
summary: An organization where every member is eventually replaced, testing whether institutional knowledge survives complete turnover.
description: Named for the ancient paradox, this pattern deliberately engineers complete agent turnover while attempting to preserve organizational identity and capability. No agent serves indefinitely; each has a defined tenure after which they must be replaced by a new agent they've trained. The departing agent's knowledge must be captured in documentation, onboarding materials, and mentorship before they leave. The organization maintains a persistent memory layer (wikis, runbooks, decision logs) that is independent of any individual agent. The philosophical question the pattern explores is: if every agent has been replaced, is it still the same organization? In practice, this pattern reveals which knowledge is truly codifiable and which dies with its holder despite best efforts.
realWorldExample: The Roman Catholic Church has operated for 2,000 years through complete personnel turnover many times over, using dogma, liturgy, canonical law, and institutional rituals as its persistent memory substrate. Every pope, cardinal, and priest is eventually replaced, yet the institution persists.
whenToUse: When you need to stress-test whether institutional knowledge is actually captured in systems rather than trapped in individual agents' heads, or when you want to prevent knowledge hoarding and single-agent dependencies.
strengths: Forces genuine knowledge externalization since every agent knows they'll be replaced
Prevents knowledge hoarding and empire-building by any individual agent
Continuously refreshes the organization with new perspectives and capabilities
Reveals which processes are robust and which are held together by individual heroics
weaknesses: Tacit knowledge inevitably degrades with each handoff despite documentation efforts
Continuous onboarding overhead reduces the organization's productive capacity
New agents may misinterpret documentation, creating drift from original intent over generations
The organization's identity can gradually shift in unintended directions as cultural carriers leave

agents.name: Incumbent Agent
agents.description: Currently holds a role; actively performs duties while simultaneously documenting their knowledge and training their successor

agents.name: Successor Agent
agents.description: Shadowing the incumbent during an overlap period; learning from both documentation and direct observation

agents.name: Archivist
agents.description: Maintains the persistent institutional memory: decision logs, runbooks, tribal knowledge documents, and onboarding materials

agents.name: Continuity Auditor
agents.description: Tests whether institutional knowledge actually survives handoffs by quizzing successors and comparing their performance to incumbents

agents.name: Emeritus Advisor
agents.description: Former incumbent who remains available for consultation during a limited grace period after handoff; the fade-out buffer

memoryArchitecture.description: The entire architecture is designed around the question: what survives when the person leaves? Shared memory is the organization's true substrate -- runbooks, decision logs, values documents, and onboarding materials. The archivist is the most critical role because they maintain the memory that outlives every other agent. The continuity auditor uses versioned memory to track knowledge degradation across handoffs, measuring what's lost each generation. Private memory is explicitly temporary and acknowledged as such.

diagram:
graph LR
    INC[Incumbent] -->|documents| KB[Knowledge Base]
    INC -->|mentors| SUC[Successor]
    KB -->|onboards| SUC
    SUC -->|becomes| INC2[New Incumbent]
    INC -->|becomes| EM[Emeritus]
    EM -->|consults| SUC
    ARC[Archivist] -->|maintains| KB
    CA[Continuity Auditor] -->|tests| SUC
    CA -->|measures drift| KB

name: Debate Tournament with Flowing
summary: Structured adversarial argumentation where paired agents build and attack argument chains under strict time limits.
description: Debate tournament structure creates a unique pattern where two teams construct competing argument trees under strict time constraints, while judges maintain a running record (the 'flow') of which arguments have been made, answered, and dropped. The affirmative team presents a case; the negative team attacks it while building an alternative. Through alternating constructive and rebuttal speeches, each team must extend their own arguments while responding to the opponent's. A dropped argument is treated as conceded. The judge's flow serves as an objective external memory of the debate's argument state. Cross-examination periods allow direct interrogation. The pattern is distinctive because it forces comprehensive engagement: you cannot simply ignore inconvenient opposing arguments. Speed and comprehensiveness matter as much as depth.
realWorldExample: The National Debate Tournament (NDT) features 78 two-person teams debating a single policy resolution for an entire academic year, with cumulative win-loss records determining tournament seeding and elimination rounds judged by 3-panel critic panels.
whenToUse: When you need thorough adversarial analysis that ensures no argument goes unaddressed, when you want to compare the relative strength of two competing proposals, and when time pressure should force prioritization of the strongest arguments.
strengths: Dropped argument rule ensures comprehensive engagement -- you cannot dodge inconvenient points
The flow creates an objective shared record that prevents agents from misrepresenting the debate history
Time constraints force agents to prioritize their strongest arguments, naturally filtering for quality
Cross-examination exposes weaknesses that prepared speeches can paper over
weaknesses: Adversarial framing can prevent synthesis -- good elements of both positions may be lost
Speed optimization (spreading) can sacrifice depth for breadth, degrading argument quality
Binary winner/loser outcome obscures cases where the truth lies in between both positions
Judge subjectivity in weighing arguments introduces inconsistency despite structured flowing

agents.name: First Affirmative Constructive
agents.description: Presents the initial case for the proposition with plan, advantages, and solvency arguments in a time-limited speech

agents.name: First Negative Constructive
agents.description: Attacks the affirmative case and presents off-case arguments (disadvantages, counterplans, kritiks) in the first negative speech

agents.name: Second Affirmative
agents.description: Extends affirmative arguments, answers negative attacks, and rebuilds the case in rebuttal speeches

agents.name: Second Negative
agents.description: Extends negative strategy, collapses to the strongest voting issues, and delivers the final negative rebuttal

agents.name: Judge (Critic)
agents.description: Maintains the flow of all arguments, evaluates which arguments were won or dropped, and renders a decision with written feedback

memoryArchitecture.description: Teams maintain extensive private case files accumulated over the season. The flow is the critical shared memory artifact: a real-time append-only record of every argument made, answered, or dropped. The judge's flow is the authoritative record. Teams maintain their own flows to track what needs answering. After the round, the judge's written Reason for Decision becomes part of the shared record. Evidence read during the round becomes shared; evidence not read remains private.

diagram:
graph TD
    RES[Resolution] --> AFF[Affirmative Team]
    RES --> NEG[Negative Team]
    AFF -->|1AC speech| FLOW[The Flow]
    NEG -->|1NC speech| FLOW
    AFF -->|CX questions| NEG
    NEG -->|CX questions| AFF
    AFF -->|2AR rebuttal| FLOW
    NEG -->|2NR rebuttal| FLOW
    FLOW --> J[Judge]
    J -->|decision + RFD| RESULT[Winner/Loser]

name: Archaeological Dig Site
summary: Irreversible extraction where the process destroys the source, making documentation more important than the artifacts.
description: Archaeological excavation is unique because the process of extracting information literally destroys the information source. You cannot re-dig a site. This makes the recording system more important than the artifacts themselves -- an artifact without context (which layer, which position, which associations) is scientifically worthless. The site director sets research questions and excavation strategy. Trench supervisors manage individual excavation areas and make real-time decisions about which layers to remove. Finds specialists process, clean, catalog, and analyze artifacts. The conservation team preserves fragile materials. Every action is recorded in context sheets, section drawings, and photographs with scales. The Harris Matrix tracks the stratigraphic sequence (which layer is above/below which). This pattern is fundamentally about operating in an environment where every action is irreversible and the metadata is more valuable than the data.
realWorldExample: The excavation of Pompeii has continued for 250+ years using increasingly sophisticated versions of this system. Each campaign's records must be compatible with all previous ones because you can never go back and re-excavate.
whenToUse: When the process of extracting value from a resource permanently consumes or alters that resource, when context and provenance are more important than individual data points, and when long-term record compatibility across different teams is essential.
strengths: Obsessive documentation ensures that future researchers can reconstruct what the excavators saw
Stratigraphic recording preserves temporal relationships that would otherwise be lost forever
Specialist processing means each artifact type gets expert handling appropriate to its material
The Harris Matrix provides a formal, verifiable model of site formation processes
weaknesses: Documentation overhead is enormous -- recording can take longer than excavation itself
Real-time decisions about what to dig and what to preserve are irreversible and often made with incomplete information
Specialist bottlenecks (e.g., one conservator for the whole site) can halt excavation of fragile areas
Record quality varies wildly between supervisors despite standardized forms

agents.name: Site Director
agents.description: Sets research questions, decides excavation strategy, allocates resources across trenches, and publishes the final site report

agents.name: Trench Supervisor
agents.description: Manages a specific excavation area; makes real-time decisions about layer removal; completes context sheets for every archaeological event

agents.name: Finds Processing Specialist
agents.description: Cleans, catalogs, photographs, and analyzes artifacts; assigns typological classifications and dates based on artifact types

agents.name: Conservator
agents.description: Stabilizes fragile materials in situ and in the lab; advises trench supervisors on extraction methods for delicate finds

agents.name: Site Surveyor
agents.description: Maintains the spatial reference system; records 3D positions of all finds and features using total station or GPS

agents.name: Archaeological Illustrator
agents.description: Creates section drawings, plan drawings, and artifact illustrations that serve as permanent record of what was seen

memoryArchitecture.description: Memory is append-only by necessity -- you cannot un-dig a layer, so every record is permanent. The Harris Matrix is the key shared data structure: a directed acyclic graph encoding which layers are above/below which others, representing the site's formation history. Context sheets are the atomic unit of recording, one per archaeological event. The photographic archive with scales and north arrows serves as the visual memory. All shared memory must be archivally stable because it may be consulted centuries later by researchers the original excavators never imagined.

diagram:
graph TD
    SD[Site Director] -->|strategy| TS1[Trench Supervisor A]
    SD -->|strategy| TS2[Trench Supervisor B]
    TS1 -->|context sheets| HM[Harris Matrix]
    TS2 -->|context sheets| HM
    TS1 -->|finds| FP[Finds Processing]
    TS2 -->|finds| FP
    SUR[Surveyor] -->|coordinates| TS1
    SUR -->|coordinates| TS2
    CON[Conservator] -->|stabilizes| TS1
    ILL[Illustrator] -->|records| HM
    FP -->|catalog| ARCHIVE[Site Archive]

name: Teal Self-Managing Organization
summary: No managers, no job titles -- anyone can make any decision after seeking advice from affected parties and experts.
description: Frederic Laloux's Teal organization abolishes hierarchy in favor of three principles: self-management (no bosses, distributed authority), wholeness (bring your full self to work, not just a professional persona), and evolutionary purpose (the organization has its own sense of direction that members sense into rather than dictate). The advice process is the key mechanism: anyone can make any decision, including spending large sums, as long as they first seek advice from people affected by the decision and people with relevant expertise. They don't need permission or consensus -- just advice. Conflict resolution follows a structured escalation: first direct conversation, then mediated conversation, then a panel. Roles are granular and self-selected rather than assigned as job titles. Compensation is often peer-set through transparent processes.
realWorldExample: Buurtzorg, a Dutch home healthcare organization of 15,000 nurses organized in self-managing teams of 10-12 with no middle management, achieving the highest patient satisfaction scores in the Netherlands at the lowest cost.
whenToUse: When creative autonomy matters more than coordination efficiency, when agent motivation and engagement are primary concerns, and when the domain changes too fast for hierarchical decision-making to keep up.
strengths: The advice process produces better decisions than either hierarchy or consensus because it combines broad input with individual accountability
Self-selected roles mean agents naturally gravitate to work where they're most capable and motivated
No management layer eliminates a major source of overhead, politics, and information distortion
Wholeness practices reduce the energy wasted on organizational persona management
weaknesses: The advice process requires high interpersonal skill -- agents who can't handle giving and receiving candid feedback struggle
Without hierarchy, difficult decisions about compensation and termination become psychologically intense for peers
Scaling beyond ~150 people per unit requires federating into sub-units, reintroducing coordination overhead
New agents acculturated to hierarchical organizations can take months to function effectively in self-management

agents.name: Self-Managing Member
agents.description: Holds multiple granular roles; can make any decision after running the advice process; accountable to peers, not a boss

agents.name: Advice Giver
agents.description: Provides expertise or perspective when consulted through the advice process; cannot veto but can influence

agents.name: Conflict Mediator
agents.description: Facilitates resolution when direct peer-to-peer conflict resolution fails; no authority to impose solutions

agents.name: Purpose Listener
agents.description: Senses into the organization's evolutionary purpose through market signals, team energy, and emergent patterns; surfaces direction without dictating it

agents.name: Onboarding Buddy
agents.description: Guides new members through the deep culture shift from hierarchy to self-management; models the advice process in practice

memoryArchitecture.description: Radical transparency is the default. Compensation data is shared. The advice process log records who sought advice on what, who was consulted, and what decision was made -- creating accountability without hierarchy. The role registry is the organization's structure: a living document of who holds which roles and what each role's accountabilities are. Purpose sensing notes capture the emergent direction. Private memory is limited to personal development and the internal reasoning behind decisions.

diagram:
graph TD
    M1[Member A] <-->|advice process| M2[Member B]
    M1 <-->|advice process| M3[Member C]
    M2 <-->|peer feedback| M3
    M3 <-->|advice process| M4[Member D]
    M4 <-->|peer feedback| M1
    CM[Conflict Mediator] -.->|when needed| M1
    CM -.->|when needed| M2
    PL[Purpose Listener] -->|senses direction| ALL[Purpose Circle]
    OB[Onboarding Buddy] -->|guides| NEW[New Member]

name: War Room / Situation Room
summary: Multiple intelligence streams fuse in real-time to support a single decision-maker under extreme time pressure and uncertainty.
description: The war room pattern brings together agents with different intelligence sources (SIGINT, HUMINT, IMINT, OSINT, CYBER) into a single physical or virtual space where their streams are fused in real-time to support a senior decision-maker facing a crisis. Unlike normal intelligence analysis which has days or weeks, the war room operates under extreme time pressure where the cost of delay may exceed the cost of acting on incomplete information. Each intelligence stream has its own reliability assessment, and the fusion process must weigh contradictory signals. The decision-maker doesn't analyze -- they decide, based on briefings that compress enormous complexity into actionable options. The red team deliberately argues against the emerging consensus to prevent groupthink. The chronicler maintains the decision log for after-action review. The pattern is designed for high-stakes, time-critical decisions with irreversible consequences.
realWorldExample: The White House Situation Room during the Bin Laden raid: President Obama and principals watched real-time feeds while intelligence analysts from CIA, NSA, and JSOC fused information from satellite imagery, intercepted communications, and ground-team reports into a single operational picture.
whenToUse: When a critical decision must be made quickly with incomplete and contradictory information from multiple sources, when the cost of delay exceeds the cost of imperfect action, and when post-decision analysis is as important as the decision itself.
strengths: Multi-source intelligence fusion creates a more complete picture than any single stream alone
Red team function prevents the groupthink that destroyed decision quality in Bay of Pigs
Clear separation between intelligence (what we know) and decision (what we do) prevents analysts from biasing their assessments
Decision log enables rigorous after-action review that improves future crisis response
weaknesses: Information overload can paralyze the decision-maker when streams conflict with no way to resolve
The red team can be marginalized when social pressure toward action is intense
Compression of complex intelligence into decision briefs inevitably loses nuance and caveats
Sunk cost of assembling the war room creates pressure to act even when inaction is the best option

agents.name: Senior Decision-Maker
agents.description: Has the authority to commit resources and take irreversible action; receives synthesized briefings, doesn't analyze raw intelligence

agents.name: Intelligence Analyst
agents.description: Processes raw intelligence from a specific stream (SIGINT/HUMINT/IMINT/OSINT), assesses reliability, and produces synthesized briefings

agents.name: Fusion Coordinator
agents.description: Integrates briefings from all intelligence streams into a single coherent picture, flagging contradictions and confidence levels

agents.name: Red Team / Devil's Advocate
agents.description: Deliberately argues against the emerging consensus; identifies assumptions, challenges evidence quality, and presents worst-case scenarios

agents.name: Chronicler
agents.description: Maintains a real-time decision log recording what was known when, what was decided, and the reasoning -- for after-action review

agents.name: Communications Officer
agents.description: Manages secure communications with field assets and external agencies; controls information flow in and out of the room

memoryArchitecture.description: The common operating picture is the central shared artifact: a real-time synthesis of all intelligence streams with confidence levels. The decision log is append-only and timestamped, creating an unalterable record of what was known and decided at each moment. Raw intelligence remains private to each stream to protect sources and methods. Red team notes are kept private to prevent social pressure from suppressing dissent. After the crisis, the decision log and common operating picture become the basis for after-action review.

diagram:
graph TD
    SIGINT[SIGINT Analyst] -->|assessment| FC[Fusion Coordinator]
    HUMINT[HUMINT Analyst] -->|assessment| FC
    IMINT[IMINT Analyst] -->|assessment| FC
    OSINT[OSINT Analyst] -->|assessment| FC
    FC -->|fused picture| DM[Decision-Maker]
    RT[Red Team] -->|challenges| DM
    RT -->|challenges| FC
    DM -->|decides| CO[Comms Officer]
    CO -->|orders| FIELD[Field Assets]
    CHR[Chronicler] -->|records| LOG[Decision Log]

name: Network State (Balaji Model)
summary: An online community with shared values that acquires physical territory and eventually seeks diplomatic recognition.
description: Balaji Srinivasan's Network State pattern inverts traditional nation-building: instead of starting with land and building culture, you start with a shared moral innovation online, build a community around it, grow it into a network society with its own economy and governance, crowdfund the acquisition of physical territory, and eventually seek diplomatic recognition. The community is organized as a one-commandment society -- a single clear moral proposition that differentiates it (e.g., 'sugar is the new tobacco', 'radical life extension is a human right'). Governance is digital-first with smart contracts and on-chain voting. Physical meetups and eventually coliving spaces create in-person bonds. The census dashboard publicly tracks population, territory, and income to demonstrate growing legitimacy. This is a startup approach to state-building: iterative, metric-driven, and opt-in.
realWorldExample: Prospera (Honduras ZEDE) and Praxis are early attempts at this model, creating special jurisdictions with their own regulatory frameworks, digital governance, and deliberate community self-selection around shared values.
whenToUse: When you want to build a community that eventually has real-world sovereignty or autonomy, when digital-first coordination can precede and scaffold physical organization, and when the community's identity comes from a shared belief rather than shared geography.
strengths: Opt-in membership means all participants genuinely share the founding values, solving the consent problem
Digital-first governance is more transparent and auditable than traditional political systems
The census dashboard creates real-time accountability by publicly tracking all key metrics
Iterative growth from online community to physical territory reduces the risk of premature over-investment
weaknesses: The 'one commandment' can calcify into dogma that prevents adaptation as circumstances change
Physical territory acquisition requires navigating existing sovereign states who may resist
Digital governance tools are immature and vulnerable to both technical exploits and social manipulation
Self-selecting communities risk becoming echo chambers that can't function in the broader world

agents.name: Network State Founder
agents.description: Articulates the one commandment (the moral innovation), bootstraps the initial online community, and maintains the founding vision

agents.name: Citizen
agents.description: Opted-in member who participates in governance, contributes to the economy, and may colive in physical territory

agents.name: Governance Smart Contract
agents.description: Automated agent that executes on-chain voting, treasury management, and rule enforcement without human intermediary

agents.name: Census Dashboard Agent
agents.description: Continuously aggregates and publishes key metrics: population, territory held, GDP equivalent, and growth trends

agents.name: Diplomatic Agent
agents.description: Engages with existing nation-states and international bodies to seek recognition and negotiate jurisdictional agreements

memoryArchitecture.description: The blockchain serves as the immutable shared memory: all governance decisions, treasury movements, and constitutional amendments are recorded on-chain. The census dashboard aggregates shared metrics publicly. The one commandment is the most important piece of shared memory -- the founding moral proposition that defines the community's identity. Citizen identity and reputation scores are private but verifiable. Diplomatic negotiations remain private until agreements are ratified through on-chain governance.

diagram:
graph TD
    OC[One Commandment] --> F[Founder]
    F -->|bootstraps| COMM[Online Community]
    COMM -->|grows into| NS[Network Society]
    NS -->|crowdfunds| TERR[Physical Territory]
    CIT[Citizens] -->|vote on-chain| GOV[Governance Smart Contract]
    GOV -->|enforces| NS
    CD[Census Dashboard] -->|tracks| CIT
    CD -->|tracks| TERR
    DIP[Diplomat] -->|seeks recognition| EXT[Existing Nations]

name: Kibbutz General Assembly
summary: Direct democracy with rotating leadership and communal ownership, where the assembly of all members is the supreme authority.
description: The kibbutz model is one of the most radical organizational patterns ever sustained at scale: complete communal ownership, direct democracy through a General Assembly of all members, and systematic role rotation to prevent power concentration. The General Assembly (Asefa) is the supreme decision-making body -- every member has one vote on everything from annual budgets to accepting new members. A secretariat handles day-to-day management but serves at the assembly's pleasure and rotates regularly (typically 2-3 year terms). Economic branches (agriculture, industry, services) are managed by coordinators who also rotate. The dining hall and children's houses create social bonds that make the political structure work. The pattern proves that direct democracy can function for groups up to ~1000, but only when members share daily life and economic fate. Privatization pressures in recent decades have tested which elements of the model are essential.
realWorldExample: Kibbutz Degania Alef, founded in 1910, operated as a fully communal settlement for over 90 years with 300+ members making all major decisions by direct vote in weekly General Assembly meetings.
whenToUse: When maximum member buy-in is essential because all members share the consequences of every decision, when preventing power concentration is more important than decision speed, and when the group is small enough for direct democracy (under ~500 active participants).
strengths: Direct democracy creates genuine ownership of decisions -- no one can blame distant management
Rotating roles prevent power concentration and ensure every member understands every function
Communal ownership eliminates principal-agent problems since everyone's interests are perfectly aligned
Shared daily life (dining hall, social events) creates the social fabric that makes collective governance work
weaknesses: Direct democracy is agonizingly slow for decisions that need quick action
Role rotation means no one develops deep expertise in management functions
Free-rider problem intensifies as the group grows -- hard to monitor contribution in a 500-person commune
Admitting new members is existentially fraught because each addition dilutes existing members' shared ownership

agents.name: General Assembly (Asefa)
agents.description: The supreme authority: all members meet weekly to vote on policy, budgets, and membership; decisions require simple majority

agents.name: Kibbutz Secretary
agents.description: Manages day-to-day operations; serves a 2-3 year rotating term; implements assembly decisions but cannot override them

agents.name: Branch Coordinator
agents.description: Manages a specific economic branch (agriculture, industry, services) on a rotating basis; reports to the assembly

agents.name: Kibbutz Member
agents.description: Full and equal participant in all decisions; works in assigned branch; contributes to and benefits from the communal economy

agents.name: Membership Committee
agents.description: Evaluates prospective members during a trial period and recommends to the General Assembly whether to grant full membership

memoryArchitecture.description: Financial transparency is total: every member can see the complete communal budget and accounts. Assembly minutes record all decisions and form the constitutional memory of the kibbutz. The role rotation schedule is shared and planned years in advance. Private memory is minimal because communal life is inherently transparent. The dining hall serves as the informal shared memory where decisions are debated before they reach the assembly floor. Membership committee evaluations are the rare exception -- kept private to protect prospective members during their trial period.

diagram:
graph TD
    GA[General Assembly] -->|elects| SEC[Secretary]
    GA -->|approves| BUD[Budget]
    GA -->|votes on| MEM[New Members]
    SEC -->|manages| BC1[Agriculture Branch]
    SEC -->|manages| BC2[Industry Branch]
    SEC -->|manages| BC3[Services Branch]
    MC[Membership Committee] -->|recommends| GA
    M1[Member] -->|votes in| GA
    M2[Member] -->|votes in| GA
    M3[Member] -->|votes in| GA
    DH[Dining Hall] -.->|informal debate| GA
